days.headline: Historic SpaceX IPO and US AI export controls dominate a geopolitically charged week in tech
days.summary:
🚀 SpaceX made history on June 12 with the largest IPO ever, raising $75 billion at $135 per share on the Nasdaq under ticker SPCX. The stock surged 19% on its first day, pushing the market cap to nearly $2.3 trillion and making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. The debut was oversubscribed four times over, cementing commercial space as a mainstream public-market asset class.

🔒 The US government issued an emergency directive on June 12 forcing Anthropic to immediately pull its two frontier models — Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — offline for all foreign nationals, citing national security concerns after reports that Mythos 5 had been jailbroken. It is the most aggressive AI export restriction the US has ever imposed and triggered an immediate global debate about AI sovereignty and access. Anthropic called the action a "misunderstanding" and says it is working to restore access as quickly as possible.

🤖 Google released Gemma 4, a new family of open-weight models built for advanced reasoning and agentic workflows under the Apache 2.0 license, while Microsoft simultaneously unveiled Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a multimodal open model balancing strong math and science reasoning with practical efficiency. Together they signal that the open-source frontier is closing the gap on proprietary models faster than expected, lowering the barrier for developers who want to run capable AI locally or in sovereign environments. The timing — just as US export controls bite — underscores the strategic value of truly open models.

🦠 A browser malware campaign called PromptSnatcher has been discovered silently intercepting private AI conversations from roughly 90,000 users, while a separate Python-based remote-access trojan, NarwhalRAT, is spreading via spear-phishing emails disguised as urgent security alerts. Running in parallel, 23 malicious Chrome extensions linked to the "SearchJack" campaign have been caught silently hijacking users' default search engines. The trio of campaigns marks a clear shift: attackers are increasingly targeting the AI interaction layer, not just traditional system endpoints.

🇸🇪 Swedish AI startup Lovable continued to attract strong investor interest this week, cementing its reputation as one of Europe's most-watched generative-AI tools. Stockholm's broader startup ecosystem showed a healthy funding cadence through early June, with rounds announced across health tech (Hälsa Hemma), climate (C-Green), and B2B SaaS — a sign that Nordic deep-tech investment remains resilient despite global macro uncertainty.

👀 Watch for Anthropic's negotiations with the US government to restore international access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the outcome could set a binding precedent for how frontier AI models are classified and controlled under export law. Also keep an eye on SpaceX's SPCX post-IPO trajectory as index funds and ETFs are now required to purchase shares.

days.headline: Open models close the gap; EU finalizes AI rules
days.summary: A new open-weight model matched frontier benchmarks at a fraction of the cost, reigniting the build-vs-buy debate for startups. Meanwhile, the EU published its final compliance timeline for the AI Act, phasing in high-risk obligations over 18 months with a grace period for documentation.

days.headline: Browsers gain agent APIs; a quiet week for hardware
days.summary: Chrome shipped an experimental, flag-gated on-page automation API aimed at accessibility and assistants, and GitHub added scheduled agents to Actions with budget caps and per-repo scoping. Hardware news was quiet heading into the weekend.
